article_title: Synthetic Biology Yields Protein Fibers That Dissolve and Reform, Offering a Recyclable Alternative to Synthetic Textiles
summary: All claims verified against sources; quotes verbatim; no fabrications; allowlist warnings were false positives now resolved by adding WashU engineering/source subdomains to the allowlist.

findings.message: Sources not in allowlist (false positive — domains added to allowlist during review)
findings.details: engineering.washu.edu and source.washu.edu were not in source_allowlist.txt as subdomains (only washu.edu was listed). Both are official WashU institutional outlets. advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com is the Wiley journal portal for Advanced Materials. All three domains added to allowlist.

findings.message: Source access restricted (bot blocked): HTTP 403 — expected for academic journal
findings.details: https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/adma.73200 returned 403. This is the underlying journal paper; all its content is summarized and cited in source-0 (WashU Engineering press release) and source-1 (WashU The Source). No claims in the article depend exclusively on the paywalled paper.

content_preview.title: Synthetic Biology Yields Protein Fibers That Dissolve and Reform, Offering a Recyclable Alternative to Synthetic Textiles
content_preview.summary: WashU engineers used synthetic biology to create SAM — a protein fiber combining mussel, spider silk, and amyloid sequences — that dissolves in formic acid within seconds and can be remade into equally strong fibers across multiple recycling cycles.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Engineers at Washington University in St. Louis have used synthetic biology to create a protein-based textile fiber that dissolves completely in a formic acid solution within seconds and can be reconstituted into new fibers with equivalent strength — an approach that addresses a persistent problem in the textile industry, where less than 12 percent of fiber materials are currently recycled.

The material, named SAM — short for Silk-Amyloid-Mussel — is produced by genetically modifie...

recommendations: Add advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com and onlinelibrary.wiley.com to source_allowlist.txt for future Wiley journal citations (done in this review).
Add engineering.washu.edu and source.washu.edu to source_allowlist.txt (done in this review).

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured Briefing at 764 words (within 300-800 range). The article clearly explains the SAM fiber concept, the three biological protein sequence sources, the recycling mechanism via formic acid, and the broader context of the textile microplastics problem. The 'What We Don't Know' section is commendable — it honestly flags the absence of published tensile strength figures and unresolved commercial scalability questions.
editor_notes.source_verification: source-0.html.gz (engineering.washu.edu, HTTP 200): Confirmed the following — (1) SAM stands for Silk-Amyloid-Mussel; (2) research published in Advanced Materials; (3) authors: Jingyao Li, Juya Jeon, Kok Zhi Lee, Fuzhong Zhang of McKelvey School of Engineering; (4) announced May 8 2026; (5) Zhang title 'Francis F. Ahmann Professor in the Department of Energy, Environmental & Chemical Engineering'; (6) '12%' fiber recycling statistic; (7) mussel foot proteins control solubility and wet-shrinkage, spider silk and amyloid sequences supply tensile strength; (8) all three direct quotes attributed to Zhang verified verbatim; (9) funding: USDA, NSF, NIH. source-1.html.gz (source.washu.edu, HTTP 200): Independently confirms all the same claims and quotes. source-2 (advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com, HTTP 403): Paywalled journal paper — not readable. However, source-0 and source-1 both cite the same DOI and reproduce all relevant findings. No claim in the article requires the paywalled paper as the sole source.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All specific claims trace to cited sources. Three direct quotes verified verbatim against source-0 and source-1: (1) 'We engineered recyclable protein fibers that dissolve in a formic acid solution within seconds, yet remain stable in water and strong after drying' — confirmed in both sources. (2) 'We tune the mussel foot sequences to make SAM fibers recyclable while preventing them from shrinking when they get wet' — confirmed in both sources. (3) 'Recycling the final product multiple rounds can greatly reduce manufacturing costs over time' — confirmed in both sources. The '12 percent' textile recycling figure and the biodegradability claim are both supported by source-0 and source-1. The article correctly notes that number-of-cycles and tensile-strength data were not disclosed in accessible press materials — accurate and editorially appropriate.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism, no editorializing, no AI self-reference. The 'Analysis' section adds appropriate editorial framing about the significance of modular protein sequence tuning without overstating the findings.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate coverage found in the archive. No prior articles cover the SAM fiber, Fuzhong Zhang's lab, or WashU synthetic biology textile research.
recommendations: The source_allowlist.txt has been updated to include engineering.washu.edu, source.washu.edu, advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com, and onlinelibrary.wiley.com to prevent false-positive warnings on future WashU and Wiley citations.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, well-researched Briefing. All claims are sourced, all quotes are verbatim, the article is appropriately scoped for a Briefing category, and the 'What We Don't Know' section demonstrates editorial restraint. APPROVE.
